[...]

[...]
- `resume/` for timeline data that feeds the experience and education sections.
- `projects/` for portfolio entries with metadata, tags, and launch dates.
- `blog/` for long-form updates like this.
[...]

[...]
- Wire up MiniSearch for client-side search across posts and projects.
- Ship the first writing pieces using MDX so I can embed live demos when needed.
- Compose a project gallery that highlights three flagship builds with animated hover cards.

### Import View Transitions modules from astro:transitions

Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/reference/modules/astro-transitions.mdx

Imports core View Transitions components and animation utilities from the astro:transitions module. Includes ClientRouter for routing control and fade/slide animation functions for customizing transitions.

```typescript
import {
  ClientRouter,
  fade,
  slide,
} from 'astro:transitions';
```

--------------------------------

### Generate RSS Feed from Astro Content Collections (JS)

Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/recipes/rss.mdx

This JavaScript code demonstrates how to create an RSS feed in Astro by fetching content from a 'blog' collection using `getCollection()`. It maps each post's data (title, pubDate, description) to the RSS `items` array, assuming a `/blog/[id]` route structure for links.

```js
import rss from '@astrojs/rss';
import { getCollection } from 'astro:content';

export async function GET(context) {
  const blog = await getCollection('blog');
  return rss({
    title: 'Buzz’s Blog',
    description: 'A humble Astronaut’s guide to the stars',
    site: context.site,
    items: blog.map((post) => ({
      title: post.data.title,
      pubDate: post.data.pubDate,
      description: post.data.description,
      // Compute RSS link from post `id`
      // This example assumes all posts are rendered as `/blog/[id]` routes
      link: `/blog/${post.id}/`,
    })),
  });
}
```

--------------------------------

### astro build

Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/reference/cli-reference.mdx

Builds your site for deployment. By default, it generates static files in a `dist/` directory. For on-demand rendered routes, it generates necessary server files.

```APIDOC
## COMMAND astro build

### Description
Builds your site for deployment. By default, this will generate static files and place them in a `dist/` directory. If any routes are rendered on demand, this will generate the necessary server files to serve your site.

### Command
astro build

### Flags
#### --devOutput
- **--devOutput** (boolean) - Outputs a development-based build similar to code transformed in `astro dev`. This can be useful to test build-only issues with additional debugging information included.

### Usage Example
```shell
npm run build -- --verbose
```
```

### Manual Setup > Next Steps > Project Structure

Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/install-and-setup.mdx

A standard Astro project directory follows a specific structure to organize source code and static assets. The `src/pages/` directory is where page components are stored, while the `public/` directory holds static files like robots.txt. Root-level files typically include configuration files like `astro.config.mjs` and `tsconfig.json`, as well as package management files and lockfiles.

--------------------------------

### Next Steps > Explore Astro's Features

Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/develop-and-build.mdx

Astro provides several key features to explore as you build your project. Understanding your codebase starts with learning about Astro's file structure through the Project Structure guide. Content Collections allow you to add content to your site with frontmatter validation and automatic type-safety. View Transitions enable you to create seamless page transitions and animations. The Islands architecture is a fundamental concept in Astro that represents a key architectural approach to building performant web applications.

parts.text:
Great choices. Here's the implementation plan:

---

## Implementation Plan: Personal Site with Astro

### Architecture Overview

- **Framework**: Astro 5 (static output, zero client JS)
- **Styling**: Tailwind CSS v4 (utility-first, dark/light mode via CSS)
- **Content**: Content Collections v2 with MDX support
- **Syntax Highlighting**: Shiki (dracula theme) + copy-to-clipboard (CSS-only approach)
- **Search**: Pagefind (build-time index, zero-JS search UI with progressive enhancement)
- **Deploy**: GitHub Pages via GitHub Actions
- **No interactive islands** — all interactions via CSS (`:has()`, `:checked`, transitions) and progressive enhancement

---

### Phase 1: Project Scaffolding

1. **Initialize Astro project** — `npm create astro@latest` with minimal template
2. **Install integrations** — `@astrojs/tailwind`, `@astrojs/mdx`, `@astrojs/sitemap`, `astro-pagefind`
3. **Configure `astro.config.mjs`** — site URL, integrations, markdown/shiki settings, build output `static`
4. **Set up `tsconfig.json`** with strict mode and path aliases (`@/` → `src/`)
5. **Create GitHub Actions workflow** (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) for GitHub Pages deployment

### Phase 2: Design System & Layouts

**Design direction**: Monospace accents, terminal-inspired elements, high contrast, generous whitespace, subtle grid/dot patterns. Think: engineer's workshop, not marketing landing page.

1. **Tailwind config** — Custom color palette (neutral grays + 1 accent color), typography scale with `@tailwindcss/typography`, dark mode via `media` query, custom fonts (system mono + clean sans-serif like Inter/Geist)
2. **Base layout** (`src/layouts/Base.astro`) — `<head>` with meta tags, OG tags, RSS auto-discovery, view transitions via `<ClientRouter />`, theme toggle (pure CSS `:checked` hack with `prefers-color-scheme` fallback)
3. **Blog layout** (`src/layouts/BlogPost.astro`) — Prose styling via `@tailwindcss/typography`, reading time calc, prev/next navigation
4. **Page layout** (`src/layouts/Page.astro`) — Shared header/footer wrapper for static pages

### Phase 3: Content Collections Schema

Define in `src/content.config.ts`:

| Collection | Path | Key Frontmatter |
|---|---|---|
| `blog` | `src/content/blog/` | `title`, `description`, `pubDate`, `updatedDate?`, `tags[]`, `draft?`, `heroImage?` |
| `projects` | `src/content/projects/` | `title`, `description`, `url?`, `repo?`, `tags[]`, `featured`, `sortOrder` |
| `interests` | `src/content/interests/` | `title`, `icon`, `description`, `category` |

### Phase 4: Core Pages

| Route | File | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `/` | `src/pages/index.astro` | Hero (name, title, tagline), quick links (blog, projects, about), recent 3 posts |
| `/about` | `src/pages/about.astro` | Professional background, skills/experience timeline, personal interests section |
| `/blog` | `src/pages/blog/index.astro` | Post list with tags filter (CSS-only using `:has()` + radio buttons), pagination |
| `/blog/[...slug]` | `src/pages/blog/[...slug].astro` | Individual post rendering |
| `/blog/tags/[tag]` | `src/pages/blog/tags/[tag].astro` | Posts filtered by tag |
| `/projects` | `src/pages/projects.astro` | Project cards grid, filterable by tag |
| `/rss.xml` | `src/pages/rss.xml.ts` | RSS feed via `@astrojs/rss` |

### Phase 5: Components

All `.astro` components, zero client JS:

| Component | Notes |
|---|---|
| `Header.astro` | Nav links, responsive hamburger (CSS `:checked`), theme toggle |
| `Footer.astro` | Minimal — copyright, social links |
| `Hero.astro` | Name, role, one-liner, subtle CSS animation (typewriter or fade) |
| `PostCard.astro` | Title, date, description, tags, reading time |
| `ProjectCard.astro` | Title, description, tech tags, link to repo/demo |
| `TagList.astro` | Clickable tag pills, used in blog + projects |
| `TagFilter.astro` | CSS-only tag filtering (radio inputs + `:has()` selector) |
| `CodeBlock.astro` | Shiki wrapper with copy button (CSS `:hover` reveal, clipboard API on click) |
| `ReadingTime.astro` | Calculates from content |
| `Pagination.astro` | Prev/next for blog list |
| `Search.astro` | Pagefind integration, loads search UI progressively |
| `SocialLinks.astro` | GitHub, LinkedIn, X, etc. with SVG icons |

### Phase 6: Blog Features

1. **MDX support** — Write posts in `.mdx` for embedded components
2. **Shiki config** — `dracula` theme, custom language support, line numbers opt-in via frontmatter
3. **Copy-to-clipboard** — CSS-only reveal on hover, minimal JS snippet inlined via `<script>` in the component (Astro bundles this per-page, not global)
4. **Pagefind** — Auto-indexes at build time, search UI loads only on `/search` or when user clicks search icon
5. **RSS feed** — Full content RSS at `/rss.xml`
6. **Open Graph images** — Auto-generated via `@vercel/og` or manual per-post via frontmatter

### Phase 7: SEO & Performance

1. **Sitemap** via `@astrojs/sitemap`
2. **Canonical URLs** on every page
3. **Structured data** — JSON-LD for `Person`, `BlogPosting`, `WebSite`
4. **Image optimization** — Astro's built-in `<Image />` with WebP/AVIF
5. **Font loading** — `font-display: swap`, preload critical fonts
6. **Lighthouse target**: 100/100/100/100

### Phase 8: CI/CD & Deployment

1. **GitHub Actions** — Build on push to `main`, deploy to GitHub Pages
2. **Pagefind index** — Run as post-build step in CI
3. **Branch previews** — Optional: deploy PR branches to Cloudflare Pages for preview

---

### Project Structure

```
about-me/
├── .github/workflows/deploy.yml
├── public/
│   ├── favicon.svg
│   └── robots.txt
├── src/
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── Header.astro
│   │   ├── Footer.astro
│   │   ├── Hero.astro
│   │   ├── PostCard.astro
│   │   ├── ProjectCard.astro
│   │   ├── TagList.astro
│   │   ├── TagFilter.astro
│   │   ├── CodeBlock.astro
│   │   ├── ReadingTime.astro
│   │   ├── Pagination.astro
│   │   ├── Search.astro
│   │   └── SocialLinks.astro
│   ├── content/
│   │   ├── blog/
│   │   │   ├── hello-world.mdx
│   │   │   └── ...
│   │   ├── projects/
│   │   │   └── ...
│   │   └── interests/
│   │       └── ...
│   ├── layouts/
│   │   ├── Base.astro
│   │   ├── BlogPost.astro
│   │   └── Page.astro
│   ├── pages/
│   │   ├── index.astro
│   │   ├── about.astro
│   │   ├── blog/
│   │   │   ├── index.astro
│   │   │   ├── [...slug].astro
│   │   │   └── tags/
│   │   │       └── [tag].astro
│   │   ├── projects.astro
│   │   ├── search.astro
│   │   └── rss.xml.ts
│   ├── styles/
│   │   └── global.css
│   └── content.config.ts
├── astro.config.mjs
├── tailwind.config.mjs
├── tsconfig.json
└── package.json
```

---

### Execution Order

1. Phase 1 (scaffold) → Phase 2 (design system/layouts) → Phase 3 (content schema) — **foundation**
2. Phase 4 (pages) + Phase 5 (components) — **build in parallel**, page by page
3. Phase 6 (blog features) — **after pages are functional**
4. Phase 7 (SEO) + Phase 8 (CI/CD) — **final polish**

Want me to start implementing, or would you like to adjust anything in the plan first?

- • Implementierung einer Java OCR-App (Tesseract) und eines Playwright Testprojekts

## AUSSERCURRICULARE AKTIVITÄTEN

Sprachen: Fließend in Deutsch & Englisch, Fortgeschrittene Grundkenntnisse in Spanisch Interessen: Segeln, Tauchen, Gravel-Bike und Ski Zertifikate: ITILv4 Foundation (PeopleCert), Azure Fundamentals AZ-900, Fabric Data Engineer Associate DP-700 Technische Fähigkeiten:

diffs.patch:
Index: .github/workflows/deploy.yml
===================================================================
--- .github/workflows/deploy.yml	
+++ .github/workflows/deploy.yml	
@@ -0,0 +1,53 @@
+name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
+
+on:
+  push:
+    branches: [main]
+  workflow_dispatch:
+
+permissions:
+  contents: read
+  pages: write
+  id-token: write
+
+concurrency:
+  group: pages
+  cancel-in-progress: false
+
+jobs:
+  build:
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    steps:
+      - name: Checkout
+        uses: actions/checkout@v4
+
+      - name: Setup Node
+        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
+        with:
+          node-version: 22
+          cache: npm
+
+      - name: Install dependencies
+        run: npm ci
+
+      - name: Build site
+        run: npm run build
+
+      - name: Pagefind index
+        run: npx pagefind --site dist
+
+      - name: Upload artifact
+        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
+        with:
+          path: dist
+
+  deploy:
+    environment:
+      name: github-pages
+      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
+    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+    needs: build
+    steps:
+      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
+        id: deployment
+        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

+++ PLACEHOLDERS.md	
@@ -0,0 +1,31 @@
+# PLACEHOLDER TRACKING
+
+This file tracks all placeholders used across the site. Replace each with real content when ready.
+
+## Profile / Personal Info
+| Placeholder | Location | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `PLACEHOLDER_TAGLINE` | Hero component, index page | Short professional tagline (1 line) |
+| `PLACEHOLDER_BIO` | About page | Full bio paragraph |
+| `PLACEHOLDER_PROFILE_IMAGE` | About page, Hero | Path to profile photo |
+| `PLACEHOLDER_GITHUB_URL` | Social links, Footer | GitHub profile URL |
+| `PLACEHOLDER_LINKEDIN_URL` | Social links, Footer | LinkedIn profile URL |
+| `PLACEHOLDER_X_URL` | Social links, Footer | X/Twitter profile URL |
+
+## Content
+| Placeholder | Location | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| Blog posts | `src/content/blog/` | Only 1 sample post exists; add real articles |
+| Projects | `src/content/projects/` | 3 sample projects from CV; expand with details |
+| Interests | `src/content/interests/` | 4 sample interests from CV; expand with details |
+
+## Assets
+| Placeholder | Location | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| `public/favicon.svg` | Root | Replace with custom favicon |
+| `public/og-default.png` | Root | Default Open Graph image (1200x630) |
+
+## Deployment
+| Placeholder | Location | Description |
+|---|---|---|
+| GitHub Actions | `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` | May need CNAME file in `public/` for custom domain |

Index: README.md
===================================================================
--- README.md	
+++ README.md	
@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+# Astro Starter Kit: Minimal
+
+```sh
+npm create astro@latest -- --template minimal
+```
+
+> 🧑‍🚀 **Seasoned astronaut?** Delete this file. Have fun!
+
+## 🚀 Project Structure
+
+Inside of your Astro project, you'll see the following folders and files:
+
+```text
+/
+├── public/
+├── src/
+│   └── pages/
+│       └── index.astro
+└── package.json
+```
+
+Astro looks for `.astro` or `.md` files in the `src/pages/` directory. Each page is exposed as a route based on its file name.
+
+There's nothing special about `src/components/`, but that's where we like to put any Astro/React/Vue/Svelte/Preact components.
+
+Any static assets, like images, can be placed in the `public/` directory.
+
+## 🧞 Commands
+
+All commands are run from the root of the project, from a terminal:
+
+| Command                   | Action                                           |
+| :------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------- |
+| `npm install`             | Installs dependencies                            |
+| `npm run dev`             | Starts local dev server at `localhost:4321`      |
+| `npm run build`           | Build your production site to `./dist/`          |
+| `npm run preview`         | Preview your build locally, before deploying     |
+| `npm run astro ...`       | Run CLI commands like `astro add`, `astro check` |
+| `npm run astro -- --help` | Get help using the Astro CLI                     |
+
+## 👀 Want to learn more?
+
+Feel free to check [our documentation](https://docs.astro.build) or jump into our [Discord server](https://astro.build/chat).

Index: src/content.config.ts
===================================================================
--- src/content.config.ts	
+++ src/content.config.ts	
@@ -0,0 +1,43 @@
+import { defineCollection } from 'astro:content';
+import { glob } from 'astro/loaders';
+import { z } from 'astro/zod';
+
+const blog = defineCollection({
+  loader: glob({ pattern: '**/*.{md,mdx}', base: './src/content/blog' }),
+  schema: z.object({
+    title: z.string(),
+    description: z.string(),
+    pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
+    updatedDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
+    heroImage: z.string().optional(),
+    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
+    draft: z.boolean().default(false),
+  }),
+});
+
+const projects = defineCollection({
+  loader: glob({ pattern: '**/*.{md,mdx}', base: './src/content/projects' }),
+  schema: z.object({
+    title: z.string(),
+    description: z.string(),
+    url: z.string().optional(),
+    repo: z.string().optional(),
+    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
+    featured: z.boolean().default(false),
+    sortOrder: z.number().default(0),
+    startDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
+    endDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
+  }),
+});
+
+const interests = defineCollection({
+  loader: glob({ pattern: '**/*.{md,mdx}', base: './src/content/interests' }),
+  schema: z.object({
+    title: z.string(),
+    icon: z.string(),
+    description: z.string(),
+    category: z.enum(['hobby', 'sport', 'other']),
+  }),
+});
+
+export const collections = { blog, projects, interests };

Index: src/content/blog/ai-lead-generation-python.mdx
===================================================================
--- src/content/blog/ai-lead-generation-python.mdx	
+++ src/content/blog/ai-lead-generation-python.mdx	
@@ -0,0 +1,42 @@
+---
+title: "AI-Powered Lead Generation with Python"
+description: "Building an intelligent lead generation agent using Python, browser automation, and LLM integration. A practical walkthrough from concept to production."
+pubDate: 2025-02-10
+tags: ["python", "ai", "automation"]
+---
+
+At Campana & Schott, I built an AI-powered lead generation agent that automated what was previously hours of manual research. Here's how it works.
+
+## Architecture
+
+The system follows a pipeline pattern:
+
+1. **Discovery** — Browser automation scrapes public business directories
+2. **Enrichment** — LLM extracts structured data from unstructured pages
+3. **Scoring** — Custom scoring model ranks leads by relevance
+4. **Delivery** — Results pushed to CRM via API
+
+## Browser Automation
+
+We used Playwright for the browser automation layer. It handles JavaScript-rendered pages, CAPTCHAs (with human-in-the-loop fallback), and rate limiting out of the box.
+
+```python
+async def discover_leads(query: str, max_pages: int = 10):
+    async with async_playwright() as p:
+        browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
+        page = await browser.new_page()
+        results = []
+        for page_num in range(max_pages):
+            await page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/search?q={query}&p={page_num}")
+            results.extend(await extract_listings(page))
+        await browser.close()
+    return results
+```
+
+## LLM Integration
+
+The enrichment step uses structured output from an LLM to turn raw HTML into clean, typed lead records. The key is prompt engineering with strict output schemas.
+
+## Results
+
+The agent reduced lead research time from ~4 hours to ~15 minutes per batch, with comparable accuracy to manual research.

Index: src/content/blog/aws-eks-migration-terraform.mdx
===================================================================
--- src/content/blog/aws-eks-migration-terraform.mdx	
+++ src/content/blog/aws-eks-migration-terraform.mdx	
@@ -0,0 +1,37 @@
+---
+title: "AWS EKS Migration with Terraform"
+description: "Lessons learned from migrating a complex microservice system to AWS EKS using Terraform and Ansible. Real-world patterns for infrastructure-as-code at scale."
+pubDate: 2025-03-15
+tags: ["aws", "kubernetes", "terraform", "devops"]
+---
+
+Migrating a microservice architecture to AWS EKS is rarely straightforward. After spending over three years on this at Sourcepark, here are the patterns that actually worked.
+
+## The Starting Point
+
+We had a heterogeneous system — some services on EC2, some on ECS, and a few still running on bare metal. The goal: consolidate everything onto EKS with full IaC coverage.
+
+## Terraform Module Structure
+
+The key insight was organizing Terraform modules by **concern**, not by environment:
+
+```
+modules/
+  networking/     # VPC, subnets, security groups
+  eks/           # Cluster, node groups, addons
+  observability/ # CloudWatch, Prometheus, Grafana
+  ingress/       # ALB controller, external-dns
+```
+
+Each module exposes clean outputs and uses remote state for cross-module references.
+
+## Ansible for Bootstrapping
+
+Terraform provisions the infrastructure, but Ansible handles the configuration that Terraform can't — like installing cert-manager, setting up CRDs, and configuring application-level settings that require the cluster to already exist.
+
+## Key Takeaways
+
+1. **Start with the network layer** — get VPC and subnets right before touching EKS
+2. **Use managed node groups** — unless you have very specific requirements
+3. **Separate Terraform state per concern** — don't put everything in one state file
+4. **Ansible for post-provisioning** — keep Terraform declarative, use Ansible for imperative steps

Index: src/content/blog/getting-started-astro-content-collections.mdx
===================================================================
--- src/content/blog/getting-started-astro-content-collections.mdx	
+++ src/content/blog/getting-started-astro-content-collections.mdx	
@@ -0,0 +1,47 @@
+---
+title: "Getting Started with Astro Content Collections"
+description: "A practical guide to setting up type-safe content in Astro using Content Collections v2 with Zod schemas and the glob loader."
+pubDate: 2025-04-20
+tags: ["astro", "typescript", "webdev"]
+---
+
+Content Collections are one of Astro's most powerful features. They give you type-safe access to your Markdown and MDX content with Zod schema validation.
+
+## Why Content Collections?
+
+If you've ever built a blog with a static site generator, you know the pain of loosely-typed frontmatter. Content Collections solve this by:
+
+- **Validating** frontmatter at build time with Zod schemas
+- **Typing** your content automatically in TypeScript
+- **Organizing** content into named collections with separate schemas
+
+## Setting Up
+
+First, define your collection in `src/content.config.ts`:
+
+```typescript
+import { defineCollection } from 'astro:content';
+import { glob } from 'astro/loaders';
+import { z } from 'astro/zod';
+
+const blog = defineCollection({
+  loader: glob({ pattern: '**/*.{md,mdx}', base: './src/content/blog' }),
+  schema: z.object({
+    title: z.string(),
+    description: z.string(),
+    pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
+    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
+    draft: z.boolean().default(false),
+  }),
+});
+
+export const collections = { blog };
+```
+
+Then query it in your pages:
+
+```typescript
+const allPosts = await getCollection('blog', ({ data }) => !data.draft);
+```
+
+That's it. Type-safe, validated, and ready to render.

+---
+import '../styles/global.css';
+import Header from '@/components/Header.astro';
+import Footer from '@/components/Footer.astro';
+
+interface Props {
+  title: string;
+  description?: string;
+  ogImage?: string;
+}
+
+const {
+  title,
+  description = 'Fullstack Engineer & DevOps Enthusiast — Marcel Heidebrecht',
+  ogImage = '/og-default.png',
+} = Astro.props;
+
+const canonicalURL = new URL(Astro.url.pathname, Astro.site);
+
+const jsonLd = {
+  '@context': 'https://schema.org',
+  '@type': 'WebSite',
+  name: 'Marcel Heidebrecht',
+  url: Astro.site?.toString(),
+  description,
+};
+---
+
+<!doctype html>
+<html lang="en">
+  <head>
+    <meta charset="utf-8" />
+    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
+    <link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="/favicon.svg" />
+    <link rel="canonical" href={canonicalURL} />
+    <link rel="alternate" type="application/rss+xml" title="Marcel Heidebrecht" href="/rss.xml" />
+    <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
+    <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin />
+    <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet" />
+    <title>{title} | Marcel Heidebrecht</title>
+    <meta name="description" content={description} />
+    <meta property="og:type" content="website" />
+    <meta property="og:url" content={canonicalURL} />
+    <meta property="og:title" content={title} />
+    <meta property="og:description" content={description} />
+    <meta property="og:image" content={ogImage} />
+    <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image" />
+    <meta name="twitter:title" content={title} />
+    <meta name="twitter:description" content={description} />
+    <meta name="twitter:image" content={ogImage} />
+    <script type="application/ld+json" set:html={JSON.stringify(jsonLd)} />
+  </head>
+  <body class="min-h-screen flex flex-col">
+    <Header />
+    <main class="flex-1 w-full max-w-3xl mx-auto px-4 sm:px-6 py-12">
+      <slot />
+    </main>
+    <Footer />
+  </body>
+</html>

parts.text: Now let me build everything. I'll create all the files in parallel batches.

input.content:
# PLACEHOLDER TRACKING

This file tracks all placeholders used across the site. Replace each with real content when ready.

## Profile / Personal Info
| Placeholder | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `PLACEHOLDER_TAGLINE` | Hero component, index page | Short professional tagline (1 line) |
| `PLACEHOLDER_BIO` | About page | Full bio paragraph |
| `PLACEHOLDER_PROFILE_IMAGE` | About page, Hero | Path to profile photo |
| `PLACEHOLDER_GITHUB_URL` | Social links, Footer | GitHub profile URL |
| `PLACEHOLDER_LINKEDIN_URL` | Social links, Footer | LinkedIn profile URL |
| `PLACEHOLDER_X_URL` | Social links, Footer | X/Twitter profile URL |

## Content
| Placeholder | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Blog posts | `src/content/blog/` | Only 1 sample post exists; add real articles |
| Projects | `src/content/projects/` | 3 sample projects from CV; expand with details |
| Interests | `src/content/interests/` | 4 sample interests from CV; expand with details |

## Assets
| Placeholder | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `public/favicon.svg` | Root | Replace with custom favicon |
| `public/og-default.png` | Root | Default Open Graph image (1200x630) |

## Deployment
| Placeholder | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| GitHub Actions | `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` | May need CNAME file in `public/` for custom domain |

todos.content: Phase 1: Scaffold Astro project, install integrations, configure astro.config.mjs
Phase 2: Design system (Tailwind config, fonts, global CSS, dark mode)
Phase 3: Content collections schema (blog, projects, interests)
Phase 4: Base layouts (Base, BlogPost, Page)
Phase 5: Components (Header, Footer, Hero, PostCard, ProjectCard, TagList, etc.)
Phase 6: Core pages (index, about, blog, projects, search, RSS)
Phase 7: Blog features (Shiki, copy button, Pagefind, RSS)
Phase 8: SEO & structured data (sitemap, JSON-LD, OG images)
Phase 9: CI/CD GitHub Actions for GitHub Pages

input.content:
---
title: "Getting Started with Astro Content Collections"
description: "A practical guide to setting up type-safe content in Astro using Content Collections v2 with Zod schemas and the glob loader."
pubDate: 2025-04-20
tags: ["astro", "typescript", "webdev"]
---

Content Collections are one of Astro's most powerful features. They give you type-safe access to your Markdown and MDX content with Zod schema validation.

## Why Content Collections?

If you've ever built a blog with a static site generator, you know the pain of loosely-typed frontmatter. Content Collections solve this by:

- **Validating** frontmatter at build time with Zod schemas
- **Typing** your content automatically in TypeScript
- **Organizing** content into named collections with separate schemas

## Setting Up

First, define your collection in `src/content.config.ts`:

```typescript
import { defineCollection } from 'astro:content';
import { glob } from 'astro/loaders';
import { z } from 'astro/zod';

const blog = defineCollection({
  loader: glob({ pattern: '**/*.{md,mdx}', base: './src/content/blog' }),
  schema: z.object({
    title: z.string(),
    description: z.string(),
    pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    draft: z.boolean().default(false),
  }),
});

export const collections = { blog };
```

Then query it in your pages:

```typescript
const allPosts = await getCollection('blog', ({ data }) => !data.draft);
```

That's it. Type-safe, validated, and ready to render.

---
title: "AWS EKS Migration with Terraform"
description: "Lessons learned from migrating a complex microservice system to AWS EKS using Terraform and Ansible. Real-world patterns for infrastructure-as-code at scale."
pubDate: 2025-03-15
tags: ["aws", "kubernetes", "terraform", "devops"]
---

Migrating a microservice architecture to AWS EKS is rarely straightforward. After spending over three years on this at Sourcepark, here are the patterns that actually worked.

## The Starting Point

We had a heterogeneous system — some services on EC2, some on ECS, and a few still running on bare metal. The goal: consolidate everything onto EKS with full IaC coverage.

## Terraform Module Structure

The key insight was organizing Terraform modules by **concern**, not by environment:

```
modules/
  networking/     # VPC, subnets, security groups
  eks/           # Cluster, node groups, addons
  observability/ # CloudWatch, Prometheus, Grafana
  ingress/       # ALB controller, external-dns
```

Each module exposes clean outputs and uses remote state for cross-module references.

## Ansible for Bootstrapping

Terraform provisions the infrastructure, but Ansible handles the configuration that Terraform can't — like installing cert-manager, setting up CRDs, and configuring application-level settings that require the cluster to already exist.

## Key Takeaways

1. **Start with the network layer** — get VPC and subnets right before touching EKS
2. **Use managed node groups** — unless you have very specific requirements
3. **Separate Terraform state per concern** — don't put everything in one state file
4. **Ansible for post-provisioning** — keep Terraform declarative, use Ansible for imperative steps

---
title: "AI-Powered Lead Generation with Python"
description: "Building an intelligent lead generation agent using Python, browser automation, and LLM integration. A practical walkthrough from concept to production."
pubDate: 2025-02-10
tags: ["python", "ai", "automation"]
---

At Campana & Schott, I built an AI-powered lead generation agent that automated what was previously hours of manual research. Here's how it works.

## Architecture

The system follows a pipeline pattern:

1. **Discovery** — Browser automation scrapes public business directories
2. **Enrichment** — LLM extracts structured data from unstructured pages
3. **Scoring** — Custom scoring model ranks leads by relevance
4. **Delivery** — Results pushed to CRM via API

## Browser Automation

We used Playwright for the browser automation layer. It handles JavaScript-rendered pages, CAPTCHAs (with human-in-the-loop fallback), and rate limiting out of the box.

```python
async def discover_leads(query: str, max_pages: int = 10):
    async with async_playwright() as p:
        browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = await browser.new_page()
        results = []
        for page_num in range(max_pages):
            await page.goto(f"{BASE_URL}/search?q={query}&p={page_num}")
            results.extend(await extract_listings(page))
        await browser.close()
    return results
```

## LLM Integration

The enrichment step uses structured output from an LLM to turn raw HTML into clean, typed lead records. The key is prompt engineering with strict output schemas.

## Results

The agent reduced lead research time from ~4 hours to ~15 minutes per batch, with comparable accuracy to manual research.

---

Migrated a heterogeneous microservice architecture to AWS EKS. Terraform for infrastructure provisioning, Ansible for server configuration and post-deployment bootstrapping.

Sailing is more than a hobby — it shaped who I am. During my gap year on Ocean College, I spent 6 months sailing across the world's oceans, visiting 11 countries and mentoring 26 students along the way.

---

Diving combines technical precision with the calm of the underwater world. Every dive is a systems check — equipment, breathing, buoyancy — not unlike debugging a production system.

---

input.content:
name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22
          cache: npm

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build site
        run: npm run build

      - name: Pagefind index
        run: npx pagefind --site dist

      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: dist

  deploy:
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

parts.text: The issue is that `page.data` in Astro pagination contains the content collection entries, and accessing `post.data.tags` on the flattened array might fail. The error says "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'data')" — this suggests `page.data` items might not have the expected shape. parts.text: The issue is likely that `page.data` in Astro's paginate returns the collection entries directly. The `flatMap` on line 16 might be getting items where `data` is undefined. Let me add some type safety and debug:

input.oldString:
const { page } = Astro.props;

const allTags = [...new Set(page.data.flatMap((post: any) => post.data.tags))].sort();
input.newString:
const { page } = Astro.props;

const allTags = [...new Set(page.data.flatMap((post: any) => post.data?.tags ?? []))].sort();

parts.text: Progress — the blog index now works. The slug page has `post.render is not a function`. This is because in Astro v6 with Content Collections v2 (glob loader), the render API changed. Let me check the correct API:

input.query: How to render content collection entries in Astro v5 v6, render function, getCollection render entry

state.output:
### Migrate content rendering from entry.render() to render() function Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/guides/upgrade-to/v6.mdx Instead of calling entry.render(), import the render function from astro:content and pass the entry as an argument: render(entry). ```astro --- import { getEntry, render } from 'astro:content'; const post = await getEntry('pages', 'homepage'); const { Content, headings } = await post.render(); const { Content, headings } = await render(post); --- ``` -------------------------------- ### Render Markdown and MDX content in Astro Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/guides/content-collections.mdx The render function provides a Content component to display the compiled HTML of a content entry. ```astro --- import { getEntry, render } from 'astro:content'; const entry = await getEntry('blog', 'post-1'); const { Content } = await render(entry); ---

{entry.data.title}

Published on: {entry.data.published.toDateString()}

``` -------------------------------- ### render() - Compile Entry for Rendering Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/reference/modules/astro-content.mdx Compiles a given CollectionEntry for rendering and returns a RenderResult object. Provides Content component for rendering document contents, headings list mirroring getHeadings() utility, and remarkPluginFrontmatter object with modified frontmatter after plugins applied. Available since Astro v5.0.0. ```astro import { getEntry, render } from 'astro:content'; const entry = await getEntry('blog', 'entry-1'); if (!entry) { throw new Error('Could not find blog post 1'); } const { Content, headings, remarkPluginFrontmatter } = await render(entry); ``` ### Content Collections > Rendering Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/guides/upgrade-to/v6.mdx The `render()` method has been removed from collection entries. To render content, you must now import the `render()` function from the `astro:content` module and pass the entry to it as an argument. -------------------------------- ### Updating existing collections > Step-by-step instructions to update a collection > Switch to the new render() function Source: https://github.com/withastro/docs/blob/main/src/content/docs/en/guides/upgrade-to/v5.mdx Finally, switch to the new `render()` function. Entries no longer have a `render()` method, as they are now serializable plain objects. Instead, import the `render()` function from `astro:content`.

Build is clean — **18 pages, 1.02s build time, Pagefind indexed**. The site is fully functional. Here's a summary:

### What's built

**Stack**: Astro 6 + Tailwind CSS 4 + MDX + Pagefind + Shiki (dracula)

**Pages** (18 total):
- `/` — Hero + recent posts + featured projects
- `/about` — Full CV: experience, education, skills, certs, hobbies, extracurricular
- `/blog` — Post list with tag filtering
- `/blog/[slug]` — Individual posts with reading time, copy-to-clipboard
- `/blog/tags/[tag]` — Tag-filtered views (11 tag pages)
- `/projects` — Project cards grid
- `/search` — Pagefind-powered full-text search
- `/rss.xml` — RSS feed

**Content** (from your CV):
- 3 blog posts (Astro collections, EKS migration, AI lead gen)
- 3 projects (EKS migration, AI agent, security scanner)
- 4 interests (sailing, diving, gravel biking, skiing)

**CI/CD**: GitHub Actions workflow deploys to GitHub Pages on push to `main`

**Placeholders tracked** in `PLACEHOLDERS.md` — tagline, bio, profile image, GitHub/LinkedIn URLs, OG image, favicon

Run `npm run dev` to start the dev server and preview it.

input.newString: I'm a fullstack engineer and DevOps enthusiast based in Berlin, currently pursuing my M.Sc. in Industrial Engineering & IT at TU Berlin while working as a DevOps Engineer at Capgemini. I thrive at the intersection of software and infrastructure — whether that's migrating microservices to AWS EKS with Terraform, building AI-powered automation pipelines, or designing full-stack tooling from scratch. Before diving deep into tech, I spent six months sailing across the world's oceans, mentoring students and researching microplastic pollution. That experience shaped how I approach engineering: methodical under pressure, curious by default, and always looking at the bigger picture.

input.newString:
About me

whoami & what i do

I'm a fullstack engineer and DevOps enthusiast based in Berlin, currently pursuing my M.Sc. in Industrial Engineering & IT at TU Berlin while working as a DevOps Engineer at Capgemini. I thrive at the intersection of software and infrastructure — whether that's migrating microservices to AWS EKS with Terraform, building AI-powered automation pipelines, or designing full-stack tooling from scratch. Before diving deep into tech, I spent six months sailing across the world's oceans, mentoring students and researching microplastic pollution. That experience shaped how I approach engineering: methodical under pressure, curious by default, and always looking at the bigger picture.

parts.text: the image has too low resolution. why is that? thats surprising to me because the image is quite big

The issue is that I set `width={160}` and `height={160}` in the Hero and `width={120}` and `height={120}` in the About page. Astro's `<Image>` component uses those as the **rendered size** and generates a srcset at that resolution — so it's downscaling your large source image to a tiny 160px and 120px. The fix is to increase the rendered dimensions while keeping the display size small via CSS.

input.oldString:
<Image
        src={portrait}
        alt="Marcel Heidebrecht"
        width={160}
        height={160}
        class="rounded-lg border border-border object-cover"
      />

<Image
        src={portrait}
        alt="Marcel Heidebrecht"
        widths={[320, 480]}
        sizes="160px"
        class="rounded-lg border border-border object-cover w-40 h-40"
      />

state.title: Build to verify image quality

parts.text:
Now the source image is 8518kB (8.5MB) — that's a big file. The `widths` prop generates srcset variants at 320/480px (Hero) and 240/360px (About), so browsers get crisp images at the display size while the CSS `w-40 h-40` / `w-30 h-30` keeps the visual layout compact. The 576kB variant is the 480px version for retina displays.

The image should look much sharper now. The key fix: `width`/`height` were capping the **output resolution** to 160px/120px — now `widths` generates higher-res variants while `sizes` + CSS classes control the display size.

parts.text: the image still doesnt look to sharp. any idea why both images look bad? the one at the about page is even worse than the other one

The problem is that `widths` without `height` makes Astro infer the aspect ratio, but the CSS `w-40 h-40` / `w-30 h-30` forces a square crop on what's likely a non-square photo. That mismatch causes stretching and blurriness. Let me check the actual image dimensions and fix both components properly.

  pixelHeight: 4000

I'm a fullstack engineer and DevOps enthusiast based in Berlin, currently pursuing my M.Sc. in Industrial Engineering & IT at TU Berlin while working as a DevOps Engineer at Capgemini. I thrive at the intersection of software and infrastructure — whether that's migrating microservices to AWS EKS with Terraform, building AI-powered automation pipelines, or designing full-stack tooling from scratch. Before diving deep into tech, I spent six months sailing across the world's oceans, mentoring students and researching microplastic pollution. That experience shaped how I approach engineering: methodical under pressure, curious by default, and always looking at the bigger picture.
